title: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease | ANATOMIC CONSIDERATIONS | FUNCTIONS OF THE GI TRACT
items.title: Understanding Sphincter Function and Its Role in Preventing Reflux
items.content: The lower esophageal sphincter (LES) plays a crucial role in preventing gastroesophageal reflux by maintaining basal tone. This function is essential for separating the stomach from the esophagus, ensuring that ingested contents do not move backward into the esophagus.
keyPoints: The LES prevents gastroesophageal reflux through its basal tone.
Coordination of sphincter relaxation and esophageal contractions propels food into the stomach.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease | ANATOMIC CONSIDERATIONS | FUNCTIONS OF THE GI TRACT

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease | ANATOMIC CONSIDERATIONS | FUNCTIONS OF THE GI TRACT
items.title: Nutrient Absorption Dynamics in the Small Intestine
items.content: The small intestine is the primary site for nutrient absorption, facilitated by mucosal villi that maximize surface area. This region efficiently absorbs most nutrients and minerals rapidly, while bile acids and vitamin B12 are specifically absorbed in the ileum.
keyPoints: Most nutrient absorption occurs in the small intestine due to mucosal villi.
Rapid absorption of nutrients and minerals happens in the proximal intestine.
Bile acids and vitamin B12 are absorbed in the ileum.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease | ANATOMIC CONSIDERATIONS | FUNCTIONS OF THE GI TRACT

items.subtopic: EXTRINSIC MODULATION OF GUT FUNCTION | OVERVIEW OF GI DISEASES
items.title: How Stress Affects Gut Function via the Brain-Gut Axis
items.content: Stress disrupts normal gut motor, secretory, and sensory activities through the brain-gut axis. This extrinsic modulation highlights how non-volitional regions of the gut are influenced by external factors like stress.
keyPoints: The brain-gut axis alters function in gut regions not under volitional regulation.
Stress disrupts normal gut motor, secretory, and sensory activities.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · EXTRINSIC MODULATION OF GUT FUNCTION | OVERVIEW OF GI DISEASES

items.subtopic: EXTRINSIC MODULATION OF GUT FUNCTION | OVERVIEW OF GI DISEASES
items.title: Role of Mucosal Immune Pathways in Protecting the Gut
items.content: Mucosal immune pathways, including epithelial and lamina propria lymphocytes, plasma cells, and lymph node chains, prevent noxious agents from entering circulation. Paneth cells secrete antimicrobial peptides to defend against pathogens.
keyPoints: Mucosal immune pathways include epithelial and lamina propria lymphocytes and plasma cells.
Lymph node chains help prevent noxious agents from entering the circulation.
Antimicrobial peptides secreted by Paneth cells defend against pathogens.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · EXTRINSIC MODULATION OF GUT FUNCTION | OVERVIEW OF GI DISEASES

items.title: Mechanisms and Red Flags of Impaired Digestion and Absorption
items.content: GI diseases can disrupt digestion and absorption through various mechanisms, including enzyme deficiencies and organ obstructions. Lactase deficiency is a common maldigestion syndrome causing gas and diarrhea post-dairy ingestion without adverse outcomes. More severe conditions like celiac disease or pancreatic cancer lead to anemia, dehydration, electrolyte disorders, or malnutrition.
keyPoints: Lactase deficiency leads to gas and diarrhea after dairy consumption but has no adverse outcomes.
Celiac disease, bacterial overgrowth, Crohn's ileitis, and radiation enteritis cause anemia, dehydration, electrolyte disorders, or malnutrition.
Benign or neoplastic biliary obstruction impairs fat digestion.
Chronic pancreatitis or pancreatic cancer leads to impaired enzyme release and malnutrition.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · CLASSIFICATION OF GI DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics of Altered Gut Transit
items.content: GI diseases can alter gut transit through mechanical obstruction, delayed propulsion, or rapid propulsion. Esophageal occlusion often results from strictures due to acid exposure or eosinophilic esophagitis. Gastroparesis is characterized by delayed gastric emptying due to impaired antral motility. Rapid gastric emptying occurs in postvagotomy dumping syndrome.
keyPoints: Esophageal occlusion often results from strictures due to acid exposure or eosinophilic esophagitis.
Gastroparesis involves delayed gastric emptying due to impaired antral motility.
Rapid gastric emptying is seen in postvagotomy dumping syndrome.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · CLASSIFICATION OF GI DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Immune Dysregulation and Its Impact on GI Conditions
items.content: Altered immune function can lead to various inflammatory GI conditions. Celiac disease results from gluten ingestion, while eosinophilic esophagitis shows mucosal eosinophil infiltration. Ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease are types of IBD causing mucosal injury primarily in the lower gut. Dysbiosis may trigger flares in IBD, celiac disease, and IBS.
keyPoints: Celiac disease results from dietary ingestion of gluten-containing grains.
Eosinophilic esophagitis exhibits mucosal eosinophil infiltration.
Ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease cause mucosal injury primarily in the lower gut.
Dysbiosis may trigger flares in IBD, celiac disease, and IBS.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · CLASSIFICATION OF GI DISEASES (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF GI DISEASES (part 2) | SYMPTOMS OF GI DISEASE
items.title: Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction: Understanding Pathophysiology and Emotional Impact
items.content: Disorders without obvious organic abnormalities, such as IBS, functional dyspepsia, and functional heartburn, are now termed disorders of gut-brain interaction (DGBI). These conditions involve complex pathophysiological mechanisms including altered gut motor function, exaggerated visceral sensory responses, abnormal central nervous system processing of pain, and mucosal immune defects. Emotional disturbances are often significant in patients with DGBIs.
keyPoints: Disorders termed disorders of gut-brain interaction (DGBI) include IBS, functional dyspepsia, and functional heartburn.
Pathophysiology involves altered gut motor function, exaggerated visceral sensory response, abnormal CNS processing of pain, and mucosal immune defects.
Significant emotional disturbances are common in patients with DGBIs.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · CLASSIFICATION OF GI DISEASES (part 2) | SYMPTOMS OF GI DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF GI DISEASES (part 2) | SYMPTOMS OF GI DISEASE
items.title: Genetic Influences on Gastrointestinal Diseases: Recognizing Hereditary Patterns
items.content: Certain gastrointestinal diseases have hereditary components, with family members of IBD patients showing a genetic predisposition. Inherited disorders can lead to colonic, esophageal, and pancreatic malignancies. Lynch syndrome is an autosomal dominant condition increasing the risk for colon and gynecologic cancers. Familial clustering in DGBIs may be due to learned illness behavior rather than true genetics.
keyPoints: IBD patients' family members have a genetic predisposition to disease development.
Lynch syndrome increases risks for colon and gynecologic cancers.
Familial clustering observed in DGBIs might result from learned behaviors.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · CLASSIFICATION OF GI DISEASES (part 2) | SYMPTOMS OF GI DISEASE

items.title: Heartburn: Prevalence, Causes, and Pathophysiology
items.content: Heartburn is a common symptom experienced by 40% of the population. It typically results from excess gastroesophageal acid reflux but can also occur with normal acid exposure due to nonacidic reflux or heightened esophageal nerve sensitivity.
keyPoints: Heartburn affects 40% of the population.
Causes include excess gastroesophageal acid reflux, nonacidic reflux, and heightened esophageal nerve sensitivity.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · CLASSIFICATION OF GI DISEASES (part 2) | SYMPTOMS OF GI DISEASE

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH GI DISEASE | HISTORY
items.title: Symptom Timing and Profiles in GI Disease Diagnosis
items.content: Understanding the timing, profiles, and duration of symptoms is crucial for diagnosing gastrointestinal diseases. Short-duration symptoms often indicate acute conditions such as infection or inflammation, while long-standing symptoms suggest chronic issues like neoplasia or DGBIs.
keyPoints: Short-duration symptoms: acute infection, inflammation, toxin exposure, ischemia
Long-standing symptoms: chronic inflammation, neoplasia, DGBIs
Luminal obstruction symptoms vary by site (e.g., dysphagia, nausea)
Symptoms worsened by meals suggest obstructions or IBDs; relieved by eating in ulcers
Ulcer pain is intermittent over weeks to months; biliary colic has sudden onset lasting hours
Acute pancreatitis pain persists for days to weeks
Diarrhea from malabsorption improves with fasting; secretory diarrhea persists without intake
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH GI DISEASE | HISTORY

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH GI DISEASE | HISTORY
items.title: Role of Symptom Criteria in Diagnosing DGIBs
items.content: The Rome criteria are widely accepted for diagnosing DGIBs, helping to reduce unnecessary diagnostic testing. They have sensitivities and specificities ranging from 55–75% when compared against structural findings.
keyPoints: Rome criteria used for DGIB diagnosis
Sensitivities and specificities: 55–75%
Criteria help reduce unnecessary testing in high-risk patients
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH GI DISEASE | HISTORY

items.title: Identifying the Source of Abdominal Pain through Physical Examination
items.content: The physical examination is crucial in determining the source and nature of abdominal pain. Differentiating between visceral, parietal, and musculoskeletal pain can guide further diagnostic steps.
keyPoints: Visceral pain presents as generalized discomfort without significant tenderness.
Parietal pain or peritonitis shows localized pain with involuntary guarding, rigidity, or rebound tenderness.
Musculoskeletal abdominal wall pain is exacerbated by Valsalva or leg lift maneuvers.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Assessing Abdominal Distention and Bowel Sounds in Gastrointestinal Disease
items.content: Abdominal inspection and auscultation provide insights into potential gastrointestinal issues such as obstruction, neoplasm, or ascites.
keyPoints: Distention may indicate obstruction, neoplasm, or ascites.
Auscultation: Loss of bowel sounds suggests ileus; high-pitched, hyperactive sounds suggest intestinal obstruction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Laboratory Tests in Gastrointestinal Disease
items.content: Laboratory tests provide critical insights into various GI conditions. Iron-deficiency anemia suggests mucosal blood loss, while vitamin B12 deficiency indicates issues with the intestinal, gastric, or pancreatic systems. Inflammatory markers like leukocytosis and increased C-reactive proteins help identify inflammation, whereas leukopenia points to viral illnesses. Electrolyte disturbances arise from severe vomiting or diarrhea.
keyPoints: Iron-deficiency anemia suggests mucosal blood loss
Vitamin B12 deficiency results from intestinal, gastric, or pancreatic disease
Leukocytosis and increased C-reactive proteins indicate inflammation
Leukopenia is seen in viral illness
Severe vomiting or diarrhea causes electrolyte disturbances
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · TOOLS FOR PATIENT EVALUATION (part 1)

items.title: Endoscopy for GI Disease Diagnosis
items.content: Endoscopic procedures are essential for diagnosing various GI conditions. Upper endoscopy is recommended as the initial test for suspected ulcer disease, esophagitis, and neoplasm due to its ability to visualize and biopsy abnormalities. Colonoscopy is preferred for colon cancer screening and can also diagnose colitis or ileitis. Capsule endoscopy is useful for visualizing small-intestinal Crohn's disease when radiography is negative.
keyPoints: Upper endoscopy is the initial test for suspected ulcer disease, esophagitis, neoplasm
Colonoscopy is preferred for colon cancer screening and surveillance
Capsule endoscopy visualizes small-intestinal Crohn's disease with negative radiography
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · TOOLS FOR PATIENT EVALUATION (part 1)

items.title: Luminal Contents Analysis in GI Evaluation
items.content: Analysis of luminal contents aids in diagnosing specific GI conditions. Fecal Helicobacter pylori antigen testing detects active infection, while stool samples can identify bacterial pathogens and parasites. Fecal calprotectin or lactoferrin elevations indicate inflammatory conditions like IBD. Gastric acid quantification helps exclude gastrinoma.
keyPoints: Fecal Helicobacter pylori antigen testing detects active infection
Stool samples can identify bacterial pathogens and parasites
Fecal calprotectin or lactoferrin elevations indicate inflammatory conditions like IBD
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · TOOLS FOR PATIENT EVALUATION (part 1)

items.title: When to Use Radiographic Tests in Gastrointestinal Evaluation
items.content: Radiographic tests are essential for evaluating both visceral and extraluminal structures. Esophagography is the initial test for dysphagia when subtle rings, strictures, or achalasia are suspected. Fluoroscopic triple-phase swallow testing is used for oropharyngeal dysphagia. Small-bowel contrast radiology helps detect tumors, strictures, and fistulae, while also estimating intestinal transit time.
keyPoints: Esophagography excludes subtle rings, strictures, or achalasia in dysphagia cases.
Fluoroscopic triple-phase swallow testing evaluates oropharyngeal dysphagia.
Small-bowel contrast radiology detects tumors, strictures, fistulae, and estimates transit.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · TOOLS FOR PATIENT EVALUATION (part 2)

items.title: Role of PET-CT in Detecting Neuroendocrine Tumors
items.content: Positron emission tomography (PET) is used to distinguish malignant from benign diseases across several organ systems. The use of combined PET-CT techniques with DOTA-octreotate and related agents has notably improved the detection rates of neuroendocrine tumors, offering a significant advantage in diagnostic accuracy.
keyPoints: PET distinguishes malignant from benign disease in various organs.
Combined PET-CT with DOTA-octreotate enhances neuroendocrine tumor detection.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · TOOLS FOR PATIENT EVALUATION (part 2)

items.title: Nutritional Modifications in GI Diseases
items.content: Nutritional modifications can alleviate symptoms and correct pathologic defects in various gastrointestinal diseases. These include lactose restriction for lactase deficiency, liquid meals for gastroparesis, carbohydrate restrictions for dumping syndrome, avoidance of specific meats for alpha-gal syndrome, low-FODMAP diets for IBS, gluten-free diet for celiac disease, elimination diets for eosinophilic esophagitis, medium-chain triglycerides for short-gut syndrome or severe ileal disease, and enteral jejunostomy feedings for gastric dysmotility syndromes.
keyPoints: Lactose restriction benefits lactase deficiency
Liquid meals improve gastroparesis symptoms
Carbohydrate restrictions help with dumping syndrome
Low-FODMAP diets alleviate IBS symptoms
Gluten-free diet reduces mucosal inflammation in celiac disease
Elimination diets improve eosinophilic esophagitis histology and symptoms
Medium-chain triglycerides are used for short-gut syndrome or severe ileal disease
Enteral jejunostomy feedings are considered for gastric dysmotility syndromes
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · Gastrointestinal Disease | NUTRITIONAL MODIFICATION | PHARMACOTHERAPY

items.title: Pharmacotherapy in GI Diseases: Symptom Control
items.content: Prescription drugs are used to manage symptoms in many gastrointestinal diseases. These include higher-dose PPIs for GERD, vonoprazan for severe acid-related disorders, baclofen for gastroesophageal reflux, fluticasone and budesonide for eosinophilic esophagitis, sucralfate for upper gut ulcers, prokinetic drugs for gastroparesis and constipation, secretagogue drugs for refractory constipation, peripheral opioid antagonists for opioid-induced constipation, and various antidiarrheals. Antiemetics, pancreatic enzymes, octreotide, and neuromodulators are also used for specific GI conditions.
keyPoints: Higher-dose PPIs recommended when OTC preparations fail for GERD
Vonoprazan is a potassium-competitive acid blocker for severe acid-related disorders
Baclofen increases lower esophageal sphincter barrier function to reduce reflux
Fluticasone and budesonide are used in eosinophilic esophagitis management
Sucralfate serves as a cytoprotective agent for upper gut ulcers
Prokinetic drugs aid GI propulsion in gastroparesis and constipation
Secretagogue drugs treat refractory constipation
Peripheral opioid antagonists manage opioid-induced constipation
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · Gastrointestinal Disease | NUTRITIONAL MODIFICATION | PHARMACOTHERAPY

items.title: Pharmacotherapy in GI Diseases: Disease Modification
items.content: Prescription medications modify disease activity by reducing inflammation, modulating immune function, and targeting neoplasms. Immunomodulatory drugs are used for inflammatory bowel diseases (IBD), microscopic colitis, and other conditions. Biologic agents like TNF inhibitors, integrin antibodies, cytokine inhibitors, JAK inhibitors, and cell adhesion molecule blockers benefit IBD. Corticosteroids, infliximab, and cyclosporine treat acute severe ulcerative colitis. Eosinophil-depleting biologics show promise in eosinophilic disorders. H. pylori eradication is crucial for some gastric malignancies.
keyPoints: Immunomodulatory drugs used in IBD, microscopic colitis, and immune checkpoint inhibitor colitis
Biologic agents benefit IBD by targeting TNF, integrins, cytokines, JAKs, and cell adhesion molecules
Corticosteroids, infliximab, and cyclosporine treat acute severe ulcerative colitis
Eosinophil-depleting biologics are promising for eosinophilic disorders
H. pylori eradication is essential for treating some gastric malignancies
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · Gastrointestinal Disease | NUTRITIONAL MODIFICATION | PHARMACOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: THERAPIES TARGETING GUT DYSBIOSIS | THERAPEUTIC ENDOSCOPY
items.title: Antibiotics and Probiotics in Gut Dysbiosis Management
items.content: Antibiotics are used to treat various gastrointestinal conditions linked to gut dysbiosis, such as H. pylori-induced ulcers, infectious diarrhea, complicated diverticulitis, and small intestinal bacterial overgrowth. Rifaximin is effective for some cases of diarrhea-predominant IBS. Probiotics and postbiotics serve as adjunctive therapies with limited efficacy evidence.
keyPoints: Antibiotics treat H. pylori-induced ulcers, infectious diarrhea, complicated diverticulitis, small intestinal bacterial overgrowth
Rifaximin is effective for some cases of diarrhea-predominant IBS
Probiotics and postbiotics are adjunctive therapies with limited efficacy evidence
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING GUT DYSBIOSIS | THERAPEUTIC ENDOSCOPY

items.title: Therapeutic Applications of Endoscopy in Gastrointestinal Bleeding
items.content: Endoscopy serves therapeutic roles beyond diagnostics, such as stopping hemorrhage from ulcers and vascular malformations using cautery or vasoconstrictors. Hemostatic powder sprays are used for persistent GI bleeding. Techniques like endoscopic clips, banding, and injections of thrombin or cyanoacrylate manage variceal and gastric bleeding.
keyPoints: Cautery techniques and vasoconstrictor injections stop hemorrhage from ulcers and vascular malformations
Hemostatic powder sprays are used for persistent GI bleeding
Endoscopic clips, banding, and thrombin or cyanoacrylate injections manage variceal and gastric bleeding
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING GUT DYSBIOSIS | THERAPEUTIC ENDOSCOPY

items.title: When to Opt for Interventional Radiology over Endoscopy in GI Bleeding
items.content: Interventional radiology techniques, such as angiographic embolization or vasoconstriction, are beneficial when endoscopic intervention is not feasible for controlling gut bleeding. These methods can also manage mesenteric ischemia through embolectomy, stent placement, and thrombolysis.
keyPoints: Angiographic embolization or vasoconstriction decreases bleeding from gut sites not amenable to endoscopy.
Interventional radiology manages mesenteric ischemia via angiographic embolectomy, stent placement, and thrombolysis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · INTERVENTIONAL RADIOLOGY | SURGERY | PSYCHOLOGICAL APPROACHES AND PHYSICAL THERAPY

items.title: Surgical Indications for Gastrointestinal Conditions
items.content: Surgery is indicated for curing certain GI conditions like ulcerative colitis and diverticulitis when medication fails, as well as for complications of ulcers such as bleeding or obstruction. It also addresses persistent intestinal obstructions post-conservative care.
keyPoints: Surgery cures medication-unresponsive ulcerative colitis, diverticulitis, cholecystitis, appendicitis, and intraabdominal abscess.
Surgical intervention is necessary for complications like bleeding or obstruction from ulcers.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · INTERVENTIONAL RADIOLOGY | SURGERY | PSYCHOLOGICAL APPROACHES AND PHYSICAL THERAPY

options: It filters and detoxifies absorbed substances

items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with lactase deficiency?
options: Gas and diarrhea after ingesting dairy products
items.explanation: Lactase deficiency produces gas and diarrhea after consuming dairy products.

items.question: What is a common cause of gastric obstruction?
items.explanation: Gastric obstruction commonly develops from ulcer disease or cancer.

items.question: Which condition is characterized by impaired esophageal body peristalsis and incomplete lower esophageal sphincter relaxation?
items.explanation: Achalasia is characterized by impaired esophageal body peristalsis and incomplete lower esophageal sphincter relaxation.

items.question: Which type of immune dysregulation results from dietary ingestion of gluten-containing grains?
items.explanation: Mucosal inflammation in celiac disease results from dietary ingestion of gluten-containing grains.

items.explanation: Heartburn is experienced as a burning substernal sensation.

items.question: Which symptom profile suggests a diagnosis of a disorder of gut-brain interaction (DGBI)?
options: Symptoms worsened by meals and relieved by defecation.
Intermittent pain over weeks to months.
Sudden onset of severe pain lasting several hours.
items.explanation: DGBI symptoms are often worsened by meals and relieved by defecation, as seen in IBS.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH GI DISEASE | HISTORY

items.question: What is a common symptom pattern for acute pancreatitis?
options: Intermittent pain over weeks to months.
Severe pain persisting for days to weeks.
Sudden onset of severe pain lasting several hours.
items.explanation: Acute pancreatitis is characterized by severe and persistent pain that lasts from days to weeks.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Gastrointestinal Disease · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH GI DISEASE | HISTORY

Sudden onset of severe pain lasting several hours.

items.explanation: Orthostasis is produced by significant blood loss.

items.question: Which of the following treatments is specifically used for recurrent Clostridioides difficile colitis?
options: Transplanting donor feces into the colon

items.question: Which therapy has limited evidence of efficacy in treating infectious diarrhea and IBS?
options: Antibiotics for small intestinal bacterial overgrowth
Probiotics containing bacterial cultures and prebiotics
items.explanation: Probiotics and prebiotics have limited evidence of efficacy for infectious diarrhea and IBS.

items.explanation: Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography is used to relieve biliary obstruction when ERCP is contraindicated.

items.question: For which condition is gastroesophageal fundoplication performed?

items.statement: GI diseases can be localized to one organ or involve multiple sites.

items.statement: Gastric cancer is most common in the United States.

items.statement: Diarrhea from malabsorption persists without oral intake.

items.statement: Loss of bowel sounds is indicative of intestinal obstruction.
items.explanation: Loss of bowel sounds signifies ileus, not intestinal obstruction.

items.statement: CT colonography is an alternative to endoscopic mucosal biopsies for diagnosing inflammatory bowel disease.

items.assertion: The brain-gut axis can alter function in regions of the gut not under volitional control.

items.assertion: Esophageal occlusion most often results from neoplasm.

items.assertion: Hepatocellular carcinoma usually arises in the setting of chronic viral hepatitis.

items.assertion: Endoscopic mucosal resection is used to ablate Barrett's esophagus with dysplasia.
